title: Judgment and diagnosis — verify before you conclude
subtitle: Evidence first: probing premises, instruments and alarming conclusions before acting on them
aigp_intro: Five lessons, one discipline: a conclusion is not evidence, however strongly it feels like one. The act trains diagnostic judgment — probe an inherited plan before building on it, read the instrument before convicting a suspect, measure a premise before deciding on it, price an alarm against the evidence you already hold, instrument an invisible surface before theorising about it. The AIGP home is Domain IV: evaluating key factors and risks relevant to the decision to deploy (IV.A) and performing the key activities that assess the AI model and system (IV.B); one lesson sits in Domain III.C's monitoring-and-maintenance territory. The framework throughline is NIST AI RMF's MAP function — establish the real context, not the described one — and MEASURE, home of TEVV (test, evaluation, verification and validation).
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development

lessons.title: A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable — probe the load-bearing unknowns before building on them.
lessons.failure: The voice mission opened with a generous handoff brief — a predecessor session's written plan — naming Whisper-small for hearing, Kokoro-82M for speaking, the wire format, the integration points file-by-file. The temptation was to trust it and start writing the architecture decision record. The operator's correction landed first: the brief had been leaned on as a map without confirming the terrain was buildable. So before any feature code, the three load-bearing unknowns ran on the real Arc 140V GPU: `WhisperPipeline` existed and transcribed a 2.7-second clip in 0.53s; Kokoro did not run on the GPU at all — the installed onnxruntime library had no GPU execution provider — yet measured a real-time factor of 0.30, three-and-a-bit times faster than playback: the brief was wrong in mechanism, right in outcome. The probe also flushed out a pre-existing broken `optimum` exporter — a three-way `transformers 5.3.0`/`optimum 2.1.0.dev0`/`optimum-intel` version mismatch — that the brief never mentioned.
evidence: 2026-06-02 — Voice, the backend half: measuring the terrain before laying track
lessons.lesson: A plan inherited from a predecessor — or a vendor, or last quarter's you — carries assumptions that were true then, there, or never. A handoff brief is a map, not proof the terrain is passable: before building on it, probe the load-bearing unknowns on the real system. Verifying an assumption always costs less than building a phase on one that has quietly rotted. Write down the number you measured, not the one you were handed.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is misplaced confidence transfer: the better a document reads, the more its detail feels like verification — but detail only proves the author was thorough about what they believed, in their environment, at their time. Hardware, library versions and provider availability drift underneath a written plan, so its claims age silently. You prevent this by triaging before you build: separate the assumptions you could cheaply survive being wrong about from the load-bearing ones an entire phase stands on, then spend the first hour probing only the load-bearing set on the actual target system. Expect a probe to pay twice — it corrects the premise you tested, and it flushes out hazards the plan never mentioned, because touching the real system exposes what reading about it cannot. Inheriting a plan is fine; inheriting its unverified physics is not.
lessons.growth_question: The brief named the models, the package, the wire format, even the integration points file-by-file. What did all that detail actually prove about whether the plan was buildable?

stat.label: Kokoro real-time factor, CPU not GPU

lessons.aigp_text: This is the pre-adoption assessment AIGP Domain IV trains: competency IV.B, performing key activities to assess the AI model and system. Probing Whisper and Kokoro on the target hardware before committing to them exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MAP function — establishing the system's real context, capabilities and deployment environment rather than an inherited description of one — and the measurements taken are MEASURE-function TEVV evidence (test, evaluation, verification and validation) feeding the build decision.
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Inherited plans are claims, not evidence; assess the model and system in the real deployment context before building on them.

lessons.title: A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit — let the instrument decide before you reach for the fix.
lessons.failure: Tier 0's egress kill-switch — a fail-closed guard that denies outbound traffic by default — had never run live until the first real boot, and that boot was unusably slow: a photo attach sat for minutes, a voice reply queued five. The guard was the obvious suspect; a deny-by-default control starving a legitimate channel is exactly the self-inflicted denial-of-service its own architecture decision record had warned about. The pull to reach in and loosen it was real. The launcher log said otherwise: the guard armed at 22:05:59 and not a single `EgressDenied` line appeared anywhere in the file — it had refused nothing. The real bottleneck was image grounding — vision analysis running eagerly the moment a photo attaches — monopolising the app's single serialised work lane, so a voice clip captured at 22:10:39 was not handed to the transcriber until 22:15:27, then transcribed in under a second.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The slowdown the live boot found, and the log that said it wasn't mine
2026-06-04 — Off the one lane: the freeze was structural, so the fix had to be
lessons.lesson: A problem the live boot surfaces next to your change makes the change a suspect, not a culprit. Suspicion — even well-founded, design-anticipated suspicion — is a hypothesis; the instrument holds the evidence. Read the log before reaching for the fix: had suspicion been trusted over the instrument, an innocent guard would have been "fixed" and the real jam shipped untouched. The live boot tells you that something is wrong; only the log tells you what.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is proximity bias: your newest change is the most available explanation for any co-occurring symptom, and when that change is a deny-by-default security control, the story "my guard is starving the system" is so plausible it feels like a completed diagnosis. Plausibility is not causation — it only nominates a suspect. Train the reflex: before modifying anything under suspicion, ask what evidence would have to exist if the suspicion were true — here, denial lines in the log — then go look. Finding that evidence convicts; its absence acquits in minutes, and either way you acted on the instrument rather than the story. Design for this moment in advance, too: a control that records every refusal can be exonerated later; one that refuses silently can never clear its own name. Reading a log costs minutes; weakening a security control on a hunch costs the control.
lessons.growth_question: If the egress guard really had been the bottleneck, what specific evidence would the launcher log have had to contain — and what did it actually show?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["First live boot: photo attach
sits minutes, voice reply
queued five"] B["Obvious suspect: the egress
kill-switch, never run
live before"] C["Loosen the deny-by-default
guard on the hunch"] D["Read the launcher

zero EgressDenied lines
in the whole file"] F["Real jam: eager image
grounding monopolises the
single serialised lane"] G["Voice clip 22:10:39 waits
until 22:15:27, transcribes
in under a second"] H["Innocent guard weakened,
real jam ships untouched"] A --> B B -->|"wrong path"| C B -->|"right path"| D C --> H D --> E E -->|"guard acquitted"| F F --> G
lessons.aigp_text: The log that acquitted the guard is what record-keeping exists to enable: the EU AI Act, at concept level, includes record-keeping and logging among its high-risk obligations precisely so behaviour in operation can be reconstructed and faults attributed — a discipline this incident exemplifies. Diagnosing a live system from its instruments rather than its suspects is NIST AI RMF MEASURE-function work — analyzing and tracking risk with evidence — applied to Domain III.C, governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — record-keeping and logging (high-risk obligation)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Logs exist so operating faults can be attributed on evidence; a suspected control is convicted or acquitted by its records.

lessons.title: A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain.
lessons.failure: The urgency case for the at-rest encryption sprint rested on one claim: that BlarAI's two SQLite stores already held "decades of the user's private data" sitting exposed in plaintext. The architecture decision record asserted it, the sprint's Strategic Design Vision asserted it, the security roadmap asserted it, the design-gate presentation asserted it — and none had checked. The Lead Architect, flipping the ADR to ACCEPTED, finally looked at the files: `substrate.db` held 107 chunks (~400 KB), `sessions.db` held 59 sessions / 376 turns (~250 KB) — all build-phase test scaffolding. Nothing real had ever been stored. The premise was false, inherited author-to-author across four documents because it was plausible, repeated, and motivating. The honest framing was the opposite of urgency — encrypt now, before first real use, so real data is born encrypted — stronger and more defensible than the claim it replaced.
evidence: 2026-06-05 — The premise nobody checked: there were never "decades of data on disk"
lessons.lesson: A load-bearing factual premise gets verified on disk, not inherited through the review chain. When a claim is doing real work in a decision, its truth is a row count or a file size — something you check — not something you carry forward because the previous document said it. Plausible-and-repeated reads as settled fact; each author inherits it from the last, and repetition manufactures authority that verification never conferred.
lessons.growth: Review chains diffuse verification: every reader assumes someone upstream checked, so a claim no one ever tested accrues authority with each repetition — and the more motivating the claim, the safer it feels, because it justifies work everyone already wants to do. Notice the tell in yourself: when a fact is carrying urgency, scope or risk in your argument, ask where in the chain it was measured. If you cannot point at the measurement, run it — load-bearing facts about a system are usually one command away: a row count, a file size, a directory listing. Then keep the second half of this story: the corrected premise was stronger, not weaker. Honest framings tend to be, because they survive the scrutiny the inherited claim was never given. The check costs a minute; a decision premised on fiction costs its credibility.
lessons.growth_question: Four authoritative documents asserted the same premise about the data on disk. How many independent verifications does that repetition amount to?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["The claim: the stores hold
decades of the user's private
data, exposed in plaintext"] B["The encryption ADR
asserts it"] C["The sprint's Strategic
Design Vision repeats it"] D["The security roadmap
repeats it"] E["The design-gate
presentation repeats it"] F["Four assertions,
zero checks — repetition
manufactures authority"] G["Lead Architect, flipping the
ADR to ACCEPTED, checks the
files on disk"] H["All build-phase scaffolding —
nothing real ever stored"] I["Honest reframe: encrypt now,

stat.before: decades of the user's private data
stat.label: the claim vs substrate.db

lessons.aigp_text: Domain IV.A asks the practitioner to evaluate the key factors and risks relevant to a deployment decision — here, the central factor (what data was actually exposed) had been asserted through four documents and evaluated by none. Checking the stores is NIST AI RMF MAP-function work: establishing the system's real context and the risks specific to it before acting. And grounding exposure claims in the actual system is the discipline ISO/IEC 42005's AI system impact assessment formalizes — impact statements drawn from evidence, not narrative.
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Repetition across documents is not verification; a premise feeding a governance decision gets measured against the actual system.

lessons.title: Verify an alarming conclusion against the evidence you already have before you escalate it — a symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion.
lessons.failure: The #615 guest-boundary test — proving the host can reach the isolation VM over Hyper-V sockets — failed `connect(): bad family`. The symptom was read correctly: this virtual environment's Python has no `socket.AF_HYPERV`. The conclusion escalated off it was wrong: that the approach was broken, wanted a ctypes rewrite (a low-level workaround), maybe a re-scope of the #598 air-gap gate. The Lead Architect pushed back in four words — "didn't we test AF_HYPERV already?" — and he was right: the Phase-2 evidence held a real host-to-Alpine AF_HYPERV round-trip. The failure was a mundane interpreter-version gap: Python 3.11.9 predates CPython's `AF_HYPERV`; the system's 3.14 has it. The design had been sound the whole time. The same instinct, applied a beat later, paid correctly: the C2 tier's `CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED` was called test-tier — not a real mutual-TLS gap — because the stubbed-GPU tier ran the identical handshake and passed.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — The on-chip session: the alarm I withdrew, and the tiers the chip locked
lessons.lesson: An alarming red is a hypothesis about the system, not a finding. Before escalating, price it against evidence you already have — a prior validation artifact, a passing sibling tier, a one-line probe. A symptom read correctly can still yield a wrong conclusion, and an unconfirmed alarm can manufacture an architecture crisis that is not there. Proactive fixing is for confirmed defects; escalation spends the decision-maker's attention and your credibility, so verify the conclusion before spending either.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: under an alarming symptom, severity transfers from the feeling to the conclusion — "this fails here" becomes "this can never work" — and a correctly-read symptom makes the wrong conclusion feel verified, because part of your chain genuinely was. Before escalating, run two checks. First: what existing evidence bears on this — has the thing ever worked anywhere (an earlier phase's validation artifact, a passing sibling test)? If it has, your conclusion must explain that evidence too, and "the design is broken" usually cannot. Second: what one-line probe discriminates between broken design and broken environment? Run it before you write the escalation. The discipline cuts both ways — the same check, applied a beat later, correctly confirmed a different alarming failure was environmental. You owe the decision-maker a verified conclusion, not a verified symptom.
lessons.growth_question: The symptom was real — that Python truly had no AF_HYPERV. What already-existing evidence did the "design is broken" conclusion fail to explain?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["#615 guest-boundary test
fails: connect(): bad family"] B["Symptom read correctly:
this venv's Python has no
socket.AF_HYPERV"] C["Escalated conclusion: approach
broken — ctypes rewrite, maybe
re-scope the #598 gate"] D["Lead Architect, four words:
didn't we test AF_HYPERV
already?"] E["Phase-2 evidence: a real
host-to-Alpine AF_HYPERV
round-trip"] F["Real cause: Python 3.11.9
predates AF_HYPERV; the
system's 3.14 has it"] G["Design sound all along —
alarm withdrawn"] A --> B B -->|"severity transfers"| C C --> D D -->|"check held evidence"| E E --> F F --> G
lessons.aigp_text: Escalating a risk is NIST AI RMF MANAGE-function work — responding to and communicating risk — and MANAGE is only as good as the MEASURE work beneath it. The Phase-2 round-trip artifact was existing TEVV evidence (test, evaluation, verification and validation) that should have priced the alarm before it reached the decision-maker. Domain IV.A's evaluation of factors and risks relevant to the deployment decision demands exactly this: conclusions that account for the validation evidence already on record before they reshape a gate.
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Communicate confirmed risks, not alarmed hypotheses — check the conclusion against existing validation evidence before escalating.

lessons.title: A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when THREE separate conditions hold — and when one is missing, instrument the tree before theorising.
lessons.failure: A folder of graphical-interface tests had collected green and run never — marked `winui`+`slow`, they skip whenever `BlarAI.Desktop.exe` is absent, i.e. every headless run. The first time the screen drove them, six of thirteen failed: all in the harness, none in the app. The fix took three foreground runs, because Windows's UI Automation (UIA) tree — the structure automated tests search for controls — is a projection gated on three preconditions a hand-driven session hides: realization (window foreground, render pass run); peer existence (only Controls get an AutomationPeer, the adapter that makes an element findable — layout panels like `Grid` get none); and non-empty/visible projection (an empty or Collapsed `ListView` is not in the tree at all). Each run taught one precondition, mistaken for the whole; twice the screen failed a "fix" just shipped — a re-anchor onto the peer-less `RootGrid`, a self-contradictory `MessagesList.exists()` on the empty-state greeting. Runs went 7→9, 9→11, then 13/13.
evidence: 2026-06-07 — The harness that had never met the window it was driving
lessons.lesson: A WinUI element is in the UI-Automation tree only when three separate conditions hold — realization, peer existence, and non-empty/visible projection — because the tree is a projection, not the visual layout or the markup you wrote. When an element is missing, instrument the tree before theorising: dump the AutomationId-bearing descendants and the item count. Printing what the framework actually exposes is the cheapest way to stop guessing at preconditions your own hands-on sessions never let you see.
lessons.growth: The trap is hidden preconditions: the harness author's own sessions satisfy them invisibly — the window is foreground, in hand — so the mental model gets built from the markup that was written, not from the projection the automation interface actually exposes. Worse, each newly discovered precondition arrives feeling complete, so you fix one and re-offend on the next; here the screen rejected two just-shipped fixes, each itself an instance of the bug being fixed. The portable counter-move: when automation cannot find what your eyes can see, make instrumentation the first act — print what the interface exposes, and the next failure becomes reading an answer instead of theorising another guess. The wider habit: a test that has never executed against its real subject is a hypothesis, and counting it as coverage is self-deception — verify fixes with the instrument, never with your model of the framework.
lessons.growth_question: Three runs each fixed one precondition and were declared complete. What single diagnostic, added at the start, would have replaced the theorising?

flowchart TD A["GUI tests marked winui+slow:
collected green,
never once executed"] B["First screen run: six of

gated on three preconditions

before theorising"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D -->|"four still fail"| E E -->|"two still fail"| F F --> G G --> H
lessons.aigp_text: NIST's trustworthy-AI characteristic "valid and reliable" rests on assessment that actually executes — a test that collects green but never runs is assurance no one has. Driving the real window and instrumenting what the interface exposes is MEASURE-function TEVV work (test, evaluation, verification and validation), and the gap exposed is TEVV's own distinction: the harness was verified against the markup as written, but only the screen could validate it against the system doing its real job in its real context. That assessment activity is Domain IV.B's competency.
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Never-executed tests are hypotheses, not assurance; validate against the real system and instrument it rather than theorise.
